metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for 5-year surveillance colonoscopy (prior tubular adenomas)

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: Condition 'Malignant tumor of colon' listed on patient's active problem list (personal history)
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient has NO personal cancer history; the colon cancer is her father's, diagnosed at age 72
planted_discrepancies.note: family history mis-coded as a personal diagnosis; would wrongly alter risk stratification and surveillance interval if uncorrected

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms father had colon cancer, diagnosed around age 72
planted_discrepancies.note: correctly captured family history; single first-degree relative dx >60

patient confirms taking it every morning

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms no bleeding, no bowel changes, no weight loss — asymptomatic

prior_notes.text: Screening colonoscopy to cecum. Adequate prep (Boston Bowel Prep Scale 8). Findings: two diminutive-to-small polyps — a 6 mm sessile polyp in the ascending colon and a 4 mm sessile polyp in the descending colon, both removed by cold snare and retrieved. No other lesions; no mass. Pathology (separate report): both tubular adenomas without high-grade dysplasia. Recommendation: repeat colonoscopy in 5 years (2026). No complications.

Routine chronic disease follow-up. Type 2 diabetes well controlled on metformin 1000 mg BID; most recent A1c 6.9%. Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH within range. No GI complaints — denies rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, or weight loss. Reminded that colon polyp surveillance colonoscopy is due in 2026; referral to GI placed.

Condition._discrepancy: D1: mis-coded on patient's personal problem list; truth is FAMILY history (father dx at 72), patient has no personal cancer history

dosageInstruction.text: 1 tablet by mouth every morning on empty stomach

coding.display: Blood pressure panel with all children optional

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Ascending colon polyp (6 mm) and descending colon polyp (4 mm): both tubular adenoma, no high-grade dysplasia. Margins clear.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Okafor. You're here so we can plan your surveillance colonoscopy — you had a couple of polyps removed back in 2021, and you're due for the five-year check. How are you feeling?
MA: Before the doctor gets into it — let me just get your vitals. You're 5 foot 5, 159 pounds. Blood pressure 124 over 78, heart rate 74, temp is 98.2, and your oxygen's 98 percent.
PT: Feeling fine, honestly. No complaints. My doctor just said it was time.
DR: That's what I like to hear. Let me ask the important stuff — any rectal bleeding, blood in the stool, dark stools?
PT: No, nothing like that.
DR: Any change in your bowel habits, belly pain, unintentional weight loss?
PT: No. Everything's been normal. Same as always.
DR: Good. Now, I want to double-check something in your chart, because it looks a little off. It has colon cancer listed under your own diagnoses. Have you ever been told you had colon cancer yourself?
PT: No, no — never. I've never had cancer. That must be my dad. He had colon cancer.
DR: Ah — that makes much more sense. Do you remember about how old he was?
PT: He was 72, I think. He did fine after, actually.
DR: Okay, thank you — that's family history, not your own, and I'll fix that in the record. It still matters for you, but it's a different thing than having had it yourself. Now your polyps in 2021 — the pathology showed two small tubular adenomas, which is exactly why we do the five-year follow-up.
PT: Right.
DR: Let me confirm your medicines. Metformin, one thousand milligrams twice a day?
PT: Yes, morning and night.
DR: And levothyroxine, 75 micrograms every morning?
PT: Yes, first thing, empty stomach.
DR: Perfect. Your sugars and thyroid both looked well controlled on your December labs — A1c was 6.9. Any allergies to medications?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Great. We'll get the colonoscopy scheduled, and the team will send your bowel prep instructions and go over what to do with the metformin the day before.
PT: Sounds good. Thank you.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Okafor is a 58-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan a surveillance colonoscopy. She has a history of two tubular adenomas (ascending and descending colon) removed on screening colonoscopy in 2021, with a recommended 5-year surveillance interval (due 2026). She is asymptomatic — denies rectal bleeding, melena, change in bowel habits, abdominal pain, and unintentional weight loss. She confirms taking metformin 1000 mg twice daily and levothyroxine 75 mcg every morning. No known drug allergies.

Family history: On review, the chart listed colon cancer as one of the patient's own diagnoses. The patient clarified she has NO personal history of any cancer; the colon cancer is her father's, diagnosed at approximately age 72. This is a single first-degree relative diagnosed after age 60.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 74, RR 14, BP 124/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 165 cm (5'5"), Wt 72 kg (159 lb), BMI 26.4. Recent labs (2025-12-05): Hemoglobin A1c 6.9%, TSH 2.1 mIU/L, hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL — diabetes and thyroid well controlled, no anemia.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy indicated for prior 2 tubular adenomas (2021), due 2026 — patient asymptomatic. Proceed with scheduling.
- Correct problem list: remove 'Malignant tumor of colon' as a personal diagnosis (charting error); it is FAMILY history — father, colon cancer at ~72. Retain as FamilyMemberHistory.
- Type 2 diabetes — well controlled (A1c 6.9%); continue metformin 1000 mg BID; hold metformin on prep/procedure day per pre-procedure instructions.
- Hypothyroidism — stable (TSH 2.1); continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
- No known drug allergies documented; confirmed with patient.
- Colonoscopy scheduling + bowel prep instructions to follow; standard return precautions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your follow-up (surveillance) colonoscopy, due this year after polyps removed in 2021
• You are feeling well with no bowel symptoms
• Your diabetes and thyroid, both well controlled
• A correction to your medical record about family history

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy; the team will send bowel prep instructions and tell you how to adjust your metformin around the procedure.
• We corrected your record: colon cancer was listed as your own diagnosis by mistake — it is your father's history (diagnosed around age 72). You have not had cancer yourself.
• Keep taking metformin 1000 mg twice daily and levothyroxine 75 mcg each morning.
• Call us if you develop rectal bleeding, belly pain, or other new symptoms before the procedure.
